content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 18,
1993
Thomas J.
Riggs
Lozano,
Smith, et al.
2444 Main Street,
Suite 260
Fresno,
CA
91721
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93-202
Dear Mr. Riggs:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
City of Clovis Mayor Dave Lawson and Councilinember Leif Sorensen
with respect to their duties under the conflict-of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.’! In your letter,
you
provided facts concerning the interests of two other members of
the council.
However,
you specifically stated that your request
only seeks advice concerning Mayor Lawson and Councilmember
Sorensen.
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTIONS
1.
May Mayor Lawson participate in decisions concerning the
University Village Complex which is a project jointly sponsored
with the City of Clovis and his employer, the University of
California
- Fresno?
•2.
May Councilmember Sorensen participate in decisions on
the same project if his personal residence is located within 2,470
feet of the boundaries of the project?
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—202
Page Two
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Mayor Lawson may participate in decisions concerning the
University Village Complex.
His salary from the University of
California
— Freno is income from a governmental source.
Such
income is not a disqualifying source of income under the Act.
2.
Councilmember Sorensen may participate in decisions on
the project provided the decisions will not affect the fair market
value of his real property by $10,000 or more or affect the rental
value of the property by $1,000 over a 12-month period.
FACTS
The City of Clovis is considering entering into a lease
agreement with the University of California
- Fresno
(the
“University”)
and with a developer for the purpose of developing
69 acres of land owned by the University.
The proposal would
provide that the University would lease to the City 69 acres of
land,
which the City would sublease to a developer.
The City
would act as the intermediary between the University and the
developer.
The property would be developed into an “entertainment
district” and is more specifically referred to as the University
Village Complex (the “complex”).
The complex would house a
performing arts center,
an arena,
a baseball stadium,
a convention
center, theme/retail space,
and parking.
Mayor Lawson is employed with the University and is in charge
of the maintenance and operation of dormitories and other
facilities on the University campus.
Councilmember Sorensen’s personal residence is located
approximately 2,470 feet from the boundaries of the proposed
project.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial


File No.
A—93—202
Page Three
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
2
! or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
Section 87103
Accordingly,
a public official may not make, participate in
making,
or attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a
member of the official’s immediate family,
or on any of the
official’s economic interests specified in Section 87103 above.
“Business entity” as used in the Act means any organization
or enterprise operated for profit,
including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership,
firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association.
(Section 82005.)
Mayor Dave Lawson
“Income” as defined by the Act,
excludes salary and
reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state,
local,
or federal government agency.
(Section 82030(b).)
The
University of California system is a “state” agency.
Accordingly,
the salary Mayor Lawson receives from the University, is not an
economic interest under Section 87103(c).
The salary he receives
would not prohibit him from participating in decisions affecting
the University.
2/
An official’s “immediate family” includes his spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


File No.
A—93—202
Page Four
Even though he is an employee of the University, the
University is not a business entity.
He does not have an economic
interest under Section 87103(d).
Councilmember Leff Sorensen
Councilmember Sorensen’s personal residence is within 2,470
feet of the boundaries of the proposed project.
His property is
an economic interest under Section 87103 (b).
Accordingly,
he may not make, participate in the making or
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
3
!
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on his real property interests.
Since his property is not the subject of the decision,
4
! but
is located approximately 2,470 feet from the project,
any
decisions made on the project would have an indirect effect on his
property.
The indirect effect of a decision on an official’s real
property interest is material if:
(1)
The real property in which the official
has an interest,
or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries)
of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official’s real property interest.
whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
when official’s real property interest is directly involved in
a decision, disqualification is required unless the decision will
have j financial effect on the real property interest.
(Regulation 18702.1.)


File No.
A—93—202
Page Five
(2)
The decision involves construction of,
or
improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm
drainage or similar facilities,
and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive newor substantially improved services.
(3)
The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries
(or
the proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest;
or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.
Regulation 18702.3(a).
If the foreseeable financial effect of any decision will
meet the thresholds set forth in the applicable provision of
Regulation 18702.3,
Councilmember Sorensen may not participate
in decisions concerning the proposed complex.
On behalf of Councilmember Sorensen,
you obtained the
services of real estate appraiser to determine the potential
finanáial impact the project would have on his personal
residence.
The appraiser stated that,
in accordance with
Regulation 18702.3,
he applied the criteria in determining
material financial effect.
He determined that,
in his opinion,
the proposed complex would not affect the value of the single-
family residence and would not have a positive or negative
impact on the residence.
The Commission has previously advised that if an appraisal
is performed by a person qualified to perform the appraisal,
and
it is determined,
based on the Commission’s materiality
regulations, that decisions will not have a material financial
effect on the official’s property, the official may rely on the
determination.
(Stone Advice Letter,
No.
A—92—133a.)
Accordingly,
Councilmember Sorensen would not be prohibited
from participating in decisions affecting the University Village
complex provided the decisions will not affect the fair market


File No.
A—93--202
Page Six
value of his real property by $10,000 or more,
or affect the
rental value of the property by $1,000 or more.
I trust this answers your questions.
If we can be of
further assistanôe, please feel free to contact the Commission’s
Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
1
/1
JeanetteE.Turvi1
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JM/JET/jt

May Mayor Lawson participate in decisions concerning the

with the City of Clovis and his employer, the University of
California
- Fresno?
•2.
May Councilmember Sorensen participate in decisions on
the same project if his personal residence is located within 2,470
feet of the boundaries of the project?g.

2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

File No.
A—93—202
Page Two

CONCLUSION: 1.
Mayor Lawson may participate in decisions concerning the
University Village Complex.
His salary from the University of
California
— Freno is income from a governmental source.
Such
income is not a disqualifying source of income under the Act.
2.
Councilmember Sorensen may participate in decisions on
the project provided the decisions will not affect the fair market
